1:
"There was also a lot for the tribe of Manasseh; for he was the firstborn of Joseph; to wit, for Machir the firstborn of Manasseh, the father of Gilead: because he was a man of war, therefore he had Gilead and Bashan." The very brief verse notes the lot of Manasseh, the firstborn of Joseph, and Machir's portion of Gilead. The grammatical voices note the lot. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "there was also a lot for the tribe of Manasseh; for he was the firstborn of Joseph... The verse holds together the lot of Manasseh.

4:
"And they came near before Eleazar the priest, and before Joshua the son of Nun, and before the princes, saying, The LORD commanded Moses to give us an inheritance among our brethren. Therefore according to the commandment of the LORD he gave them an inheritance among the brethren of their father." The witnesses note the daughters of Zelophehad claiming and receiving their inheritance by the commandment of the LORD — the crux of the faith that claims the promise of God, and the justice of God that gives the inheritance to the believing. On "The LORD commanded Moses to give us an inheritance among our brethren... according to the commandment of the LORD he gave them an inheritance," interpreters expound the claim. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the faith that claims the promise — "and they came near before Eleazar the priest, and before Joshua... saying, The LORD commanded Moses to give us an inheritance among our brethren. Therefore according to the commandment of the LORD he gave them an inheritance among the brethren of their father": the daughters of Zelophehad came forward, pleading the word of the LORD given through Moses, and received their inheritance; the truth that the case of the daughters, decided by God through Moses, established a statute in Israel, the inheritance passing to daughters where there was no son, "the daughters of Zelophehad speak right," Numbers 27:7; The figure of the faith that claims the promise of God and the justice of God that gives the inheritance to the believing.

11:
"And Manasseh had in Issachar and in Asher Bethshean and her towns, and Ibleam and her towns, and the inhabitants of Dor and her towns, and the inhabitants of Endor and her towns, and the inhabitants of Taanach and her towns, and the inhabitants of Megiddo and her towns, even three countries." The very brief verse notes Manasseh's cities within Issachar and Asher. The grammatical voices note the cities. The verse holds together Manasseh's cities.

12:
"Yet the children of Manasseh could not drive out the inhabitants of those cities; but the Canaanites would dwell in that land." The very brief verse notes Manasseh's failure to drive out the Canaanites of those cities. The grammatical voices note the failure. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the children of Manasseh could not drive out the inhabitants of those cities; but the Canaanites would dwell in that land" — Manasseh failed to drive out the Canaanites of those strong cities; the yet the children of Manasseh could not drive out the inhabitants of those cities (could not drive out); the but the Canaanites would dwell in that land (the Canaanites remaining); the figure (the failure to drive out the foe, the Canaanites left in the land; the truth that Manasseh, like Ephraim, left the Canaanites in their strong cities; the figure of the incomplete obedience that leaves the enemy, the world tolerated, "they did not destroy the nations, concerning whom the LORD commanded them," Psalm 106:34; the Canaanites left; the failure of full obedience, vv.10). The verse holds together the failure.

13:
"Yet it came to pass, when the children of Israel were waxen strong, that they put the Canaanites to tribute; but did not utterly drive them out." The very brief verse notes Israel putting the Canaanites to tribute but not driving them out. The grammatical voices note the compromise. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "when the children of Israel were waxen strong, that they put the Canaanites to tribute; but did not utterly drive them out" — when strong enough to expel the foe, Israel chose tribute instead of full obedience; the yet it came to pass, when the children of Israel were waxen strong (when Israel grew strong); the that they put the Canaanites to tribute; but did not utterly drive them out (tribute, not driven out); the figure (Israel, strong enough to drive out the foe, choosing gain over obedience; the truth that when they could have expelled the Canaanites, they spared them for tribute; the figure of the compromise that prefers gain to obedience, the foe tolerated for profit, "covetousness, which is idolatry," Colossians 3:5; the Canaanites spared for tribute; the half-hearted obedience, vv.12). The verse holds together the compromise.

On "Yet it came to pass, when the children of Israel were waxen strong, that they put the Canaanites to tribute," the witnesses note the tribute. Gill notes Israel, grown strong, putting the foe to tribute (the gain, Colossians 3:5) — the tribute (when strong).

On "but did not utterly drive them out," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the failure: "did not utterly drive them out" — the foe not utterly driven out (the disobedience); the figure (the compromise that prefers gain to obedience); the providence (the failure, the complaint of the children of Joseph to follow, vv.14). The verse displays Israel putting the Canaanites to tribute but not driving them out.

The verse displays the children of Joseph complaining of their lot to Joshua (vv.14).
14:
"And the children of Joseph spake unto Joshua, saying, Why hast thou given me but one lot and one portion to inherit, seeing I am a great people, forasmuch as the LORD hath blessed me hitherto?" The witnesses note the complaint of the children of Joseph, that their lot was too small for so great a people — the crux of the discontent that pleads its greatness yet shrinks from the labour, the murmuring that blames others for what its own sloth has left undone. On "Why hast thou given me but one lot and one portion to inherit, seeing I am a great people," interpreters expound the complaint. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the discontent of Joseph — "and the children of Joseph spake unto Joshua, saying, Why hast thou given me but one lot and one portion to inherit, seeing I am a great people, forasmuch as the LORD hath blessed me hitherto?": the children of Joseph complained that their one lot was too small for so numerous a people; the figure — the believer who complains of his portion yet will not take the kingdom by the violence of faith and diligence, "the kingdom of heaven suffereth violence, and the violent take it by force," Matthew 11:12. The figure of the discontent that pleads its greatness yet shrinks from the labour of possessing the inheritance.

On "and the children of Joseph spake unto Joshua, saying, Why hast thou given me but one lot and one portion to inherit," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the complaint. Keil and Ellicott note the children of Joseph complaining that their portion was too small, blaming Joshua for the narrowness of their lot (the discontent, Proverbs 13:4) — the complaint (but one lot).

On "seeing I am a great people, forasmuch as the LORD hath blessed me hitherto?" the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the plea of greatness. Calvin and Gill note Joseph pleading their numbers and God's blessing, yet using it to murmur rather than to labour, the self-importance that excuses sloth, the kingdom not taken by those who will not strive (the discontent that pleads greatness, Matthew 11:12) — the figure (the believer who complains yet will not take the kingdom by violence); the providence (the complaint, Joshua's answer to clear the wood country to follow, vv.15). The verse displays the complaint of the children of Joseph that their lot was too small.

The witnesses agree the substance: the children of Joseph complained that their one lot was too small for so great a people (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of discontent: why hast thou given me but one lot — the discontent of the proud and numerous tribe, pleading their greatness and God's blessing, yet unwilling to take the pains to possess what was given (Proverbs 13:4), men pleading their dignity to excuse their sloth, a figure of the believer who will not take the kingdom by the violence of faith and diligence (Matthew 11:12), God's blessing in increase a call to greater labour, not an excuse for ease. The verse displays the discontent crux. The complaint of the children of Joseph that their lot was too small displays the crux: Why hast thou given me but one lot and one portion to inherit, seeing I am a great people, forasmuch as the LORD hath blessed me hitherto? (Proverbs 13:4; Matthew 11:12) — the discontent that pleads its greatness and God's blessing yet shrinks from the labour of possessing the inheritance, the murmuring that blames others for what its own sloth has left undone. The verse displays Joshua's answer, to get up to the wood country and clear it (vv.15).
15:
"And Joshua answered them, If thou be a great people, then get thee up to the wood country, and cut down for thyself there in the land of the Perizzites and of the giants, if mount Ephraim be too narrow for thee." The witnesses note Joshua's answer, turning their own plea against them: if they were a great people, let them clear the forest and win room — the crux of the wise reproof that bids the discontented labour for what they desire rather than murmur. On "If thou be a great people, then get thee up to the wood country, and cut down for thyself," interpreters expound the answer. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the reproof that bids them labour — "and Joshua answered them, If thou be a great people, then get thee up to the wood country, and cut down for thyself there in the land of the Perizzites and of the giants, if mount Ephraim be too narrow for thee": Joshua retorted their own argument, bidding the great people prove their greatness by clearing the forest and possessing it; the truth that the slothful are answered not with more but with a call to use what they have, the inheritance enlarged by labour and faith; the figure — the kingdom won by striving, the believer bidden to lay hold of the promise by diligence and not to murmur, "be not slothful, but followers of them who through faith and patience inherit the promises," Hebrews 6:12. The figure of the wise reproof that bids the discontented labour for what they desire rather than murmur.

On "and Joshua answered them, If thou be a great people, then get thee up to the wood country, and cut down for thyself," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the reproof. Keil and Ellicott note Joshua retorting their own argument, bidding the great people clear the forest and make room by their labour (the reproof that bids them labour, Proverbs 12:24) — the answer (get thee up and cut down).

On "there in the land of the Perizzites and of the giants, if mount Ephraim be too narrow for thee," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the call to win room. Calvin and Gill note Joshua bidding them drive out the Perizzites and the giants and win room by faith and diligence, the inheritance enlarged by labour, the kingdom won by striving (the diligence that inherits, Hebrews 6:12) — the figure (the kingdom won by striving, the believer bidden to lay hold by diligence); the providence (the answer, Joseph's plea of the iron chariots to follow, vv.16). The verse displays Joshua's answer, bidding the great people clear the forest and win room.

The verse displays Joseph's answer, that the hill was not enough and the valley had iron chariots (vv.16).
16:
"And the children of Joseph said, The hill is not enough for us: and all the Canaanites that dwell in the land of the valley have chariots of iron, both they who are of Bethshean and her towns, and they who are of the valley of Jezreel." The very brief verse notes Joseph's plea that the hill was too small and the valley held iron chariots. The grammatical voices note the plea. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "the hill is not enough for us: and all the Canaanites that dwell in the land of the valley have chariots of iron... and they who are of the valley of Jezreel" — Joseph pleaded that the hill was too narrow and the valley too strong for the iron chariots; the and the children of Joseph said, The hill is not enough for us (the hill too small); the and all the Canaanites that dwell in the land of the valley have chariots of iron, both they who are of Bethshean and her towns, and they who are of the valley of Jezreel (the iron chariots); the figure (Joseph pleading the strength of the foe, the iron chariots a terror; the truth that they shrank from the valley for fear of the chariots of iron; the figure of the unbelief that magnifies the obstacle and shrinks from the fight, the iron chariots an excuse, "the people be strong... and the cities are walled, and very great," Numbers 13:28; the chariots feared; the unbelief that draws back, vv.15). The verse holds together the plea of the chariots.

17:
"And Joshua spake unto the house of Joseph, even to Ephraim and to Manasseh, saying, Thou art a great people, and hast great power: thou shalt not have one lot only:" The very brief verse notes Joshua granting that Joseph was great and should have more than one lot. The grammatical voices note the grant. Keil and Delitzsch and Gill note that "Joshua spake unto the house of Joseph... Thou art a great people, and hast great power: thou shalt not have one lot only" — Joshua granted their greatness and power and promised them more, but by their own effort; the and Joshua spake unto the house of Joseph, even to Ephraim and to Manasseh, saying, Thou art a great people, and hast great power (great and powerful); the thou shalt not have one lot only (not one lot only); the figure (Joshua owning their greatness and power, turning it to a call to use it; the truth that their very strength was the ground for bidding them win more; the figure of the gift that calls forth the effort, the strength to be used, "to whom much is given... of him shall be much required," Luke 12:48; the greatness owned; the call to use the power, vv.15). The verse holds together the grant of greatness.

On "thou shalt not have one lot only," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the promise: "thou shalt not have one lot only" — the promise of more than one lot (the call to use their power); the figure (the gift that calls forth the effort); the providence (the grant, the charge to clear the mountain and drive out the Canaanites to follow, vv.18). The verse displays Joshua granting Joseph's greatness and the promise of more.

The verse displays the charge to clear the mountain and drive out the Canaanites despite their chariots (vv.18).
18:
"But the mountain shall be thine; for it is a wood, and thou shalt cut it down: and the outgoings of it shall be thine: for thou shalt drive out the Canaanites, though they have iron chariots, and though they be strong." The witnesses note Joshua's charge that the mountain should be theirs to clear, and they should drive out the Canaanites despite their iron chariots — the crux of the faith and diligence that overcome the strongest obstacle, the promise that what God gives is won by labour and courage. On "thou shalt drive out the Canaanites, though they have iron chariots, and though they be strong," interpreters expound the charge. The reformed Calvin and the dominant reading note the faith that overcomes — "but the mountain shall be thine; for it is a wood, and thou shalt cut it down... for thou shalt drive out the Canaanites, though they have iron chariots, and though they be strong": Joshua charged them to clear the mountain and to drive out the Canaanites despite the iron chariots they feared; the truth that the chariots of iron, the terror of the valley, were nothing against the promise of God, the same God who burned the chariots at Merom giving the victory, "some trust in chariots... but we will remember the name of the LORD," Psalm 20:7; The figure of the faith and diligence that overcome the strongest obstacle, the promise won by labour and courage.

On "but the mountain shall be thine; for it is a wood, and thou shalt cut it down: and the outgoings of it shall be thine," the grammatical-historical Keil and witnesses note the charge to clear. Keil and Ellicott note Joshua bidding them clear the wooded mountain and possess it, the inheritance won by their labour (the diligence that possesses, Psalm 20:7) — the charge (cut it down and possess).

On "for thou shalt drive out the Canaanites, though they have iron chariots, and though they be strong," the reformed Calvin and grammatical-historical note the faith that overcomes. Calvin and Gill note Joshua assuring them they should drive out the Canaanites despite the iron chariots, the obstacle no barrier to faith, the strong foe overcome where God has promised (the faith that overcomes the world, Romans 8:31; 1 John 5:4) — the figure (the faith that fears no chariot and possesses the promise); the providence (the charge, the setting up of the tabernacle at Shiloh to follow, ch.18). The verse displays Joshua's charge to clear the mountain and drive out the Canaanites despite their chariots.

The witnesses agree the substance: Joshua charged them to clear the mountain and drive out the Canaanites despite the iron chariots they feared (Calvin, Keil, Ellicott, Gill). The verse bears its weight as the crux of faith that overcomes: thou shalt drive out the Canaanites, though they have iron chariots, faith and labour together possessing the inheritance (Romans 8:31) (Psalm 20:7), a figure of the faith that overcomes the world and fears no power of the enemy (1 John 5:4), what God gives won by labour and courage. The verse displays the faith-overcomes crux. Joshua's charge to clear the mountain and drive out the Canaanites despite their chariots displays the crux: but the mountain shall be thine... for thou shalt drive out the Canaanites, though they have iron chariots, and though they be strong (Romans 8:31; Psalm 20:7; 1 John 5:4) — the faith and diligence that overcome the strongest obstacle, the iron chariots no barrier to the God who gave the land, the promise won by labour and courage, a figure of the faith that overcomes the world. The verse closes the inheritance of Joseph, before the tabernacle is set up at Shiloh (ch.18).
